title: Text as Data: Why Machines Can't Read
concept: Machine learning models are pure math and can only work with numbers, so raw text — letters, words, sentences — must first be translated into numbers before any classifier can use it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the Course Capstone churn predictor, what kind of input did the model actually use to make predictions?

options.text: Neat numeric and categorical features, like minutes watched or plan type
Raw paragraphs of customer feedback the model read like a human

exercises.explain: Exactly! Every model you've built so far, from Linear Regression to Random Forests, ran on tidy rows of numbers. But what happens when your data is just... words?

cards.title: One folder machines can't touch — yet
cards.body: You just spent 280 days turning spreadsheets into predictions. But businesses also drown in something spreadsheets don't have: raw text. Emails, reviews, support tickets, tweets. Can any of the classifiers you've learned — Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression — read a single one of them?

cards.title: What does "text as data" mean?
cards.body: Every machine learning model, no matter how it's described, is really just math underneath — multiplying and adding numbers. A model has no built-in way to understand letters or words. Before a classifier can sort text into categories, the words themselves must first be translated into numbers it can actually compute with.

cards.title: A recipe in a language you can't read
cards.body: Imagine a chef who only understands measurements — 200 grams, 350 degrees. Handed a recipe written in a language they've never seen, they're stuck, even though the recipe is perfectly good. A machine learning model is that chef: brilliant with numbers, completely stuck on raw words until someone translates.

cards.title: The spam filter's hidden step
cards.body: Back in Unit 37, Naive Bayes learned to label an email "spam" or "not spam" using probability. But Naive Bayes never actually reads a sentence — it only ever sees numbers that represent the words. Something had to build those numbers first. That missing step is exactly what this unit teaches.

cards.title: The model isn't "reading" anything
cards.body: It's tempting to picture a spam filter reading your email the way you do — understanding sentences, tone, sarcasm. In reality, classical text classifiers never touch a single letter directly. They only ever see the numeric translation of the text, produced by a separate step before the model ever runs.

cards.title: Numeric Data vs. Text Data
cards.body: Why one is model-ready and the other isn't:

left.text:
Rows of numbers
Ready for a model
e.g. signup_days: 42

right.text:
Raw letters and words
Needs translation first
e.g. "Please cancel my plan"

exercises.prompt: Why can't a Logistic Regression model use a raw customer email as input directly?

options.text: Logistic Regression only computes with numbers, and raw text isn't numbers
Logistic Regression can only make predictions on weekends
Emails are always too long for any model to process

exercises.explain: Right — every classifier you've learned is fundamentally a math formula. It needs numeric input, and raw text has to be converted first.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Naive Bayes understands the meaning of a sentence the same way a human reader does.
exercises.explain: False. Naive Bayes never reads meaning — it only ever calculates probabilities from numbers that represent words, not the words themselves.

exercises.sentence: Before a classifier can sort text into categories, the words must first be translated into ___.
exercises.explain: Text has to become numbers before any classical machine learning model — including ones you've already met — can work with it.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is actually ready to be fed straight into a classifier like Logistic Regression?

options.text: [3, 0, 1, 0, 2] — a list of numbers representing a document
"Your package has shipped!" — a raw sentence
A photo of a shipping label

exercises.explain: A list of numbers is exactly the kind of input a classical ML model needs. The raw sentence still needs a translation step first.

exercises.prompt: A support team wants to use the Logistic Regression classifier you learned about back in Unit 25 to automatically route incoming customer emails. Before that model can make a single prediction, what has to happen first?

options.text: The raw email text must be converted into some numeric representation the model can actually compute with
Nothing — Logistic Regression can read the email text exactly as written
The email has to be translated into a different human language first
The support team needs to build an entirely new kind of classifier just for text

exercises.explain: Exactly! The classifier itself doesn't need to change at all — Logistic Regression is still Logistic Regression. What has to happen first is turning those words into numbers. Next up: the simplest way to do exactly that.
